result.markdown:
--- title: "Host-Virus Interaction Networks" subtitle: "Species interaction networks from GBIF and GLOBI; potyvirus host range in ecological context" author: "chrissi m." date: last-modified format: html: toc: true code-fold: true execute: echo: true warning: false message: false --- ## Introduction To check whether interactions between species harbouring viral flanking sequences could explain the distribution of homologous loci, I built multilayer networks connecting: 1. **Species–host plant** ecological interactions (from EPPO / GLOBI) 2. **Shared-host** projections (species sharing a common host) 3. **Phylogenetic** relationships (Open Tree of Life) See also the interactive [BioNexus Shiny app](../shiny/bionexus/) which queries GBIF and GLOBI in real time. ## Part 1: Bipartite species–host network ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} species_hosts <- list( "Fallopia convolvulus" = character(0), "Luperina nickerlii" = c("Elytrigia juncea", "Poaceae grasses"), "Luperina testacea" = c("Poaceae grasses", "Dactylis glomerata"), "Trichiura crataegi" = c("Prunus spinosa", "Crataegus", "Salix", "Carpinus"), "Sphenella marginata" = c("Senecio/Jacobaea ragworts") ) edges <- enframe(species_hosts, name = "species", value = "hosts") %>% unnest(hosts, keep_empty = TRUE) host_nodes <- edges$hosts[!is.na(edges$hosts)] bip_graph <- graph_from_data_frame( d = edges %>% filter(!is.na(hosts)), vertices = tibble( name = c(names(species_hosts), unique(host_nodes)), type = c( rep(FALSE, length(species_hosts)), rep(TRUE, length(unique(host_nodes))) ) ), directed = FALSE ) stopifnot(!any(is.na(V(bip_graph)$type))) ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ggraph(bip_graph, layout = "bipartite") + geom_edge_link() + geom_node_point(aes(color = type), size = 6) + geom_node_text(aes(label = name), repel = TRUE, size = 4) + scale_color_manual(values = c("FALSE" = "steelblue", "TRUE" = "darkorange"), labels = c("species", "host plant")) + theme_void() + ggtitle("Species–Host Bipartite Network") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Bipartite virus–host network: the two node classes (viral species, orange; host plants, blue) occupy the two rows, and an edge joins a virus to a plant it infects. Hubs are generalist viruses (or widely-infected hosts).](05b_networks_files/figure-html/plot-bipartite-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} proj <- bipartite_projection(bip_graph) species_graph <- proj$proj1 ggraph(species_graph, layout = "fr") + geom_edge_link() + geom_node_point(color = "steelblue", size = 8) + geom_node_text(aes(label = name), repel = TRUE, size = 4) + theme_void() + ggtitle("Species-only Projected Network") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![One-mode projection of the bipartite network onto the viral species: two viruses are linked if they share at least one host plant. Edges now represent ecological overlap (shared host range) rather than direct infection.](05b_networks_files/figure-html/species-projection-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: # Part 2: Multilayer network with phylogenetic and ecological edges ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} species <- names(species_hosts) eco_edges <- enframe(species_hosts, name = "species", value = "hosts") %>% unnest(hosts, keep_empty = TRUE) hosts <- unique(eco_edges$hosts[!is.na(eco_edges$hosts)]) ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Match species names to OTT IDs via the Open Tree of Life API (network). # Wrapped so the chapter still renders offline / if the service is down — # phylo_edges then falls back to empty and the multilayer network simply # omits the phylogenetic layer. phylo_edges <- tryCatch({ taxa <- rotl::tnrs_match_names(species) ott_ids <- taxa$ott_id[!is.na(taxa$ott_id)] tree <- rotl::tol_induced_subtree(ott_ids = ott_ids) tibble( from = tree$tip.label[tree$edge[, 1]], to = tree$tip.label[tree$edge[, 2]] ) %>% filter(!is.na(from), !is.na(to)) %>% mutate(type = "phylogenetic", weight = 1) }, error = function(e) { message("Open Tree of Life query unavailable (", conditionMessage(e), "); omitting the phylogenetic layer.") tibble(from = character(), to = character(), type = character(), weight = numeric()) }) ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} pair_grid <- expand.grid(sp1 = species, sp2 = species, stringsAsFactors = FALSE) shared_host_edges <- pair_grid %>% filter(sp1 < sp2) %>% mutate( shared = map2_int(sp1, sp2, ~ length(intersect( species_hosts[[.x]], species_hosts[[.y]]))) ) %>% filter(shared > 0) %>% transmute(from = sp1, to = sp2, type = "shared_host", weight = shared) ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} eco_edges2 <- eco_edges %>% filter(!is.na(hosts)) %>% transmute(from = species, to = hosts, type = "ecological", weight = 1) all_edges <- bind_rows(eco_edges2, phylo_edges, shared_host_edges) ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} traits <- tibble( name = c(species, hosts), trophic = case_when( name %in% species ~ "herbivore", name %in% hosts ~ "plant", TRUE ~ "unknown"), mobility = case_when( name %in% species ~ "flying", name %in% hosts ~ "sessile", TRUE ~ "unknown"), taxon = case_when( name %in% species ~ "Insecta", name %in% hosts ~ "Plantae", TRUE ~ "internal") ) ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} nodes <- traits %>% transmute(id = name, label = name, group = trophic, mobility = mobility, taxon = taxon) edges_vis <- all_edges %>% transmute( from, to, value = weight, title = paste(type, "weight =", weight), color = case_when( type == "ecological" ~ "darkorange", type == "phylogenetic" ~ "forestgreen", type == "shared_host" ~ "steelblue")) visNetwork(nodes, edges_vis, height = "900px", width = "100%") %>% visEdges(smooth = FALSE) %>% visOptions(highlightNearest = TRUE, nodesIdSelection = TRUE) %>% visLegend() %>% visPhysics(stabilization = TRUE) %>% visLayout(randomSeed = 123) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` Interactive multilayer virus–host–interaction network. Edge colour encodes the relationship type (orange/green/blue for the layers combined above); drag nodes to explore, hover for taxonomic details. Built from GBIF/GLOBI records plus Open Tree taxonomy. ::: ::: ## BioNexus Shiny App The BioNexus v3.0 Shiny application provides an interactive interface for querying GBIF and GLOBI interaction data for arbitrary species lists and constructing multilayer networks with multiplex participation coefficients. The app lives at [`shiny/bionexus/app.R`](../shiny/bionexus/app.R). To launch: ```r shiny::runApp(here::here("shiny", "bionexus")) ``` Key features: - GBIF occurrence mapping for user-supplied species - GLOBI biotic interaction retrieval - Multilayer network construction (ecological + phylogenetic + shared-host layers) - Multiplex participation coefficient for identifying bridge species - Interactive visualisation via `visNetwork` ### A saved BioNexus network: *Zucchini yellow mosaic virus* host community A live Shiny app cannot be embedded in a static document (an interactive `shinylive`/WASM build is a future option). Instead, the network below is **reconstructed from a saved BioNexus session** — the GLOBI-derived biotic interaction network built around a *Zucchini yellow mosaic virus* (a cucurbit potyvirus) host list. **Hover a node** for its taxonomy and a **PhyloPic silhouette**, resolved to the most specific rank PhyloPic provides (species → genus → family → order → class) so the outline matches the organism rather than a generic class icon. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} sess <- readRDS(here::here("data", "bionexus_zucchini95.rds")) # base64-embed cached PhyloPic silhouettes (per taxon, most specific rank # available) into hover tooltips — looked up by node name phylodir <- here::here("data", "phylopic") sil_b64 <- function(taxon) { if (is.na(taxon) || !nzchar(taxon)) return("") f <- file.path(phylodir, paste0(gsub("[^A-Za-z0-9]", "_", taxon), ".png")) if (!file.exists(f)) return("") sprintf('', base64enc::base64encode(f)) } # Keep the meaningful biotic interaction layers; the dense "cooccurrence" layer # (a statistical association, ~85% of edges) is dropped so the network is not a # hairball. Nodes left with no biotic edge (orphans) are removed. biotic <- sess$edges_df %>% dplyr::filter(layer != "cooccurrence") keep <- unique(c(biotic$from_name, biotic$to_name)) nodes <- sess$nodes_df %>% dplyr::filter(name %in% keep) %>% dplyr::mutate( label = name, group = functional_group, title = mapply(function(nm, k, cl, or, fa) paste0( "
", sil_b64(nm), "", nm, "
", k, " · ", cl, "
", or, " · ", fa, "
"), name, kingdom, class, order, family, SIMPLIFY = TRUE)) id_of <- stats::setNames(sess$nodes_df$id, sess$nodes_df$name) edges <- biotic %>% dplyr::transmute( from = id_of[from_name], to = id_of[to_name], title = paste0(layer, " (", confidence, ")"), color = dplyr::recode(layer, pollination = "#e67e22", herbivory = "#27ae60", parasitism = "#c0392b", predation = "#8e44ad", .default = "#95a5a6")) %>% dplyr::filter(!is.na(from), !is.na(to)) visNetwork(nodes, edges, height = "620px", width = "100%") %>% visNodes(size = 12, font = list(size = 13), borderWidth = 1, color = list(border = "#ffffff")) %>% visEdges(smooth = FALSE, color = list(opacity = 0.45), width = 0.6) %>% # Live force-directed physics (spring/bounce) rather than a frozen igraph layout: # forceAtlas2 gives a lively but self-settling arrangement; drag a node to see it # spring back into place. visPhysics(solver = "forceAtlas2Based", forceAtlas2Based = list(gravitationalConstant = -60, centralGravity = 0.012, springLength = 90, springConstant = 0.08, damping = 0.4, avoidOverlap = 0.5), stabilization = list(enabled = TRUE, iterations = 200), maxVelocity = 30, minVelocity = 0.75) %>% visOptions(highlightNearest = list(enabled = TRUE, degree = 1, hover = TRUE), nodesIdSelection = TRUE, selectedBy = "group") %>% visLayout(randomSeed = 7) %>% visLegend(main = "Functional group", width = 0.18, position = "right") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` **Ecological interaction network of the *Zucchini yellow mosaic virus* host community** (Cucurbitaceae), reconstructed from a saved BioNexus session that queried GBIF + GLOBI. 46 taxa are shown, connected by 567 recorded biotic interactions across the herbivory/parasitism/pollination/predation layers (edge colour = layer). The dense co-occurrence layer is omitted and isolated nodes removed for legibility; node colour = functional group, layout = force-directed (Fruchterman–Reingold), hover a node for its taxonomy and a PhyloPic silhouette. ``` ::: ::: Silhouettes are fetched once with the rOpenSci `rphylopic` package (`scripts/fetch_phylopics.R`) and cached, so the chapter renders offline [@gearty2023]. ## Summary The network analysis places the species harbouring viral-homologous loci within a connected ecological network. Pollinators and their host plants — including known potyvirus hosts — cluster together, consistent with the hypothesis that viral flanking sequences were transferred horizontally via a shared biological vector during co-infection events.
